title: After Helping Dozens Install It: The Three Most Asked Questions About OpenClaw
summary: OpenClaw security concerns center on asset value—the platform has improved default permissions and requires manual authorization for risky plugins, with daily updates addressing community-reported vulnerabilities. Security decisions should consider what's actually at risk on your machine, while cost evaluation depends on your specific use case and computational needs.
content:
I've helped dozens of people install OpenClaw remotely, and there are three questions I get asked the most: Is it safe? Is it expensive? And should I choose Claude Code or OpenClaw? Let's address them here.
I wrote a separate post about security before; here are some new updates.
OpenClaw now releases a new version every day. Version 3.8 came out the day before yesterday, fixing all the issues the community previously discussed regarding excessive permissions and Feishu (Lark) pairing. The default permissions are set conservatively—you can configure it to prevent file deletion or modification, and the permission documentation is quite clear.
For example, in February, someone installed an older version where Feishu private messaging was open by default—if you created a bot in your organization, anyone could click on it and chat with it, effectively controlling your computer. That was indeed dangerous. Now the Feishu plugin requires pairing approval by default, and the first connection requires manual authorization on your machine.
But security ultimately comes down to assets. Attacking you takes effort; there's opportunity cost and risk of getting caught. If what's stolen isn't worth more than these costs, no one will come after you.
So first ask yourself: what's actually on your computer? If it's just personal daily files, don't be too anxious. But consider: are you using a work computer? Your personal stuff might not be valuable, but company information might be important to your employer—that's something to think about. Take it seriously when needed: buy a separate machine for physical isolation, choose better models. The heavier the assets, the more you should spend on protection—just like buying a security door.
Keep in sync with official updates. This is more important than anything else.
I see a lot of "it burns too much money" comments. I'm not quite sure which kind of "expensive" people are referring to.

title: Hardening Linux: Guía de Seguridad y Bastionado de Servidores
summary: Linux hardening es un conjunto de técnicas para mejorar la seguridad de sistemas Linux, reduciendo la superficie de ataque y minimizando vulnerabilidades explotables. El proceso incluye mantener sistemas actualizados, implementar gestión de identidades robusta, configurar firewalls, y utilizar sistemas de control de acceso como SELinux.
content:
El Linux hardening es un proceso crítico para fortalecer la seguridad de los sistemas operativos Linux, reduciendo su superficie de ataque y minimizando vulnerabilidades.
El Linux hardening, también conocido como bastionado Linux o endurecimiento de sistemas Linux, es un conjunto de técnicas y prácticas diseñadas para mejorar la seguridad de los sistemas operativos Linux.
Los objetivos principales del Linux hardening son:
Reducir la superficie de ataque del sistema
Minimizar las vulnerabilidades explotables
Fortalecer la configuración predeterminada
Implementar el principio de mínimo privilegio
Mejorar la auditoría y el monitoreo del sistema
El concepto de hardening de sistemas no es nuevo, pero ha ganado relevancia crítica en la era de las amenazas cibernéticas sofisticadas. Con el aumento de los ataques dirigidos y el crecimiento exponencial de la infraestructura basada en Linux, la necesidad de implementar prácticas de hardening se ha vuelto fundamental para las organizaciones de todos los tamaños.
El proceso de Linux hardening implica varios pasos y técnicas:
Detección del sistema: Mantener el sistema y todas las aplicaciones actualizadas con los últimos parches de seguridad.


Detección de usuarios y permisos: Implementar una gestión de identidades y acceso robusta para controlar quién puede acceder al sistema y qué pueden hacer.


Detección de firewall: Utilizar herramientas como iptables o ufw para controlar el tráfico de red.


Detección de servicios innecesarios: Eliminar o deshabilitar servicios y aplicaciones que no sean esenciales para el funcionamiento del sistema.

Detección de SELinux o AppArmor: Utilizar estos sistemas de control de acceso obligatorio para reforzar la seguridad del sistema.

content:
Prometheus y Grafana: Monitoreo Avanzado para DevOps 2025


Prometheus y Grafana son herramientas fundamentales para el monitoreo en entornos DevOps modernos. Juntas, ofrecen una solución potente para recopilar métricas, visualizar datos y configurar alertas, mejorando significativamente la observabilidad de sistemas complejos.
Fundamentos de Prometheus y Grafana
Configuración e implementación
Integración con Kubernetes
Creación de dashboards efectivos
Configuración de alertas
Mejores prácticas y optimizaciones
Prometheus es un sistema de monitoreo de código abierto diseñado para la recopilación y almacenamiento de métricas en tiempo real. Grafana, por su parte, es una plataforma de visualización que permite crear dashboards interactivos a partir de diversas fuentes de datos, incluyendo Prometheus.
Características clave de Prometheus y Grafana:
Modelo de datos multidimensional
Lenguaje de consulta flexible (PromQL)
Arquitectura pull para recopilación de métricas
Visualizaciones personalizables y potentes
Soporte para múltiples fuentes de datos
Capacidades de alerta configurables
Prometheus fue desarrollado originalmente por SoundCloud en 2012 como respuesta a la necesidad de un sistema de monitoreo más flexible y escalable. Grafana surgió poco después, en 2014, como una alternativa de código abierto a las soluciones de visualización existentes.
La combinación de Prometheus y Grafana se ha convertido en un estándar de facto para el monitoreo en entornos cloud-native, especialmente en ecosistemas Kubernetes. Su adopción ha crecido exponencialmente debido a su flexibilidad, potencia y gran comunidad de usuarios.
El flujo básico de monitoreo con Prometheus y Grafana se puede resumir en los siguientes pasos:
Prometheus recopila métricas de los targets configurados (aplicaciones, servidores, etc.)
Las métricas se almacenan en una base de datos de series temporales
Prometheus evalúa reglas de alerta basadas en las métricas recopiladas
Grafana consulta los datos de Promethe

content:
CI/CD GitHub Actions: Automatización avanzada para DevOps


CI/CD con GitHub Actions es una potente herramienta de automatización integrada en GitHub que permite a los equipos de desarrollo implementar flujos de trabajo de integración y despliegue continuo directamente desde sus repositorios. Esta solución simplifica la configuración de pipelines, acelera el ciclo de desarrollo y mejora la calidad del software.
CI/CD con GitHub Actions es una plataforma de automatización que permite a los desarrolladores crear, probar y desplegar código directamente desde GitHub. Esta herramienta integra perfectamente los procesos de integración continua (CI) y despliegue continuo (CD) en el flujo de trabajo de desarrollo, eliminando la necesidad de sistemas externos.
Las principales características de CI/CD con GitHub Actions incluyen:
Configuración basada en YAML
Amplia biblioteca de acciones predefinidas
Integración nativa con repositorios GitHub
Soporte para múltiples lenguajes y plataformas
Ejecución en contenedores o máquinas virtuales
GitHub Actions fue lanzado en 2018 como una respuesta a la creciente demanda de herramientas de CI/CD integradas en plataformas de control de versiones. Antes de su introducción, los equipos de desarrollo a menudo dependían de soluciones externas como Jenkins o Azure DevOps para implementar sus pipelines de CI/CD.
El funcionamiento de CI/CD con GitHub Actions se basa en workflows definidos en archivos YAML dentro del repositorio. Estos workflows especifican los eventos que los desencadenan, los jobs a ejecutar y los pasos dentro de cada job.
Un workflow típico de CI/CD con GitHub Actions podría incluir:
Activación por push a una rama específica
Construcción del código
Ejecución de pruebas automatizadas
Generación de artefactos
Despliegue a entornos de staging o producción
name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v2
    - name: Set up Node.

title: 15 Claude Code Skills That Replace Repetitive Dev Workflows (with free /review skill)
summary: Claude Code Skills are reusable slash commands saved as markdown files that automate common development workflows, eliminating repetitive prompt copying. The article provides a free /review skill for comprehensive code review across security, performance, error handling, type safety, conventions, and edge cases.
content:
If you use Claude Code, you might not know about Skills — they're reusable slash commands you create once and use forever. Think of them as saved workflows.
I built 15 of them for common dev tasks after a year of daily Claude Code usage. Here's the full /review skill for free — it's the one I use most.
/review Skill (Free — Take It)


Create this file at .claude/skills/review.md:
# Skill: Code Review

Review the staged changes (or specified files) for:

## Checklist
1. **Security** — SQL injection, XSS, auth bypass, exposed secrets, CORS issues
2. **Performance** — N+1 queries, missing indexes, unnecessary re-renders, unbounded loops
3. **Error handling** — Uncaught promises, missing try/catch, silent failures, generic error messages
4. **Type safety** — Any types, missing null checks, incorrect generics, type assertions
5. **Conventions** — Naming consistency, file organization, import ordering, dead code
6. **Edge cases** — Empty arrays, null values, concurrent access, large inputs, unicode

## Output Format
For each issue found:
- 🔴 **Critical** — Must fix before merge
- 🟡 **Warning** — Should fix, could cause problems
- 🟢 **Suggestion** — Nice to have, improves quality

## Commands
- `git diff --cached` to review staged changes
- If no staged changes, review files modified in the last commit

Now type /review in Claude Code and get a comprehensive code review instantly. No more copy-pasting instructions every time.
Before skills, my workflow looked like this:
Open a notes app
Find the right prompt
Copy it
Paste into Claude Code
Modify for context
Run it
With skills:
Type /review

Done.
Skills are markdown files in .claude/skills/ that Claude Code loads automatically. They define a complete workflow — what to check, how to check it, and how to format the output.

title: How to build a free HN data pipeline in 30 minutes
summary: This tutorial demonstrates building a lightweight Hacker News data pipeline using Apify's free tier to automatically fetch, store, and analyze top stories. The guide covers setup, SQLite storage, and basic trend detection—all without cost. It enables tracking how topics rise on HN over time for market and sentiment research.
content:
Hacker News is one of the richest sources of signal in tech. New frameworks, hiring waves, shifting sentiment — it all shows up on HN before it hits mainstream. But scraping HN yourself is tedious and fragile.
In this tutorial, I'll walk you through building a lightweight data pipeline that pulls structured HN data on a schedule, stores it locally, and runs basic trend detection — all for free.
We'll use the HN Top Stories actor on Apify, which returns clean JSON for top, new, best, ask, show, and job stories. It handles pagination, rate limits, and retries so you don't have to.
Apify's free tier gives you enough compute to run this daily without paying a cent.
Install the Apify client:
pip install apify-client

Then pull the latest top stories:
from apify_client import ApifyClient
from datetime import datetime

client = ApifyClient("YOUR_APIFY_TOKEN")

run = client.actor("cryptosignals/hn-top-stories").call(
    run_input={"category": "topstories", "maxItems": 100}
)

items = list(
    client.dataset(run["defaultDatasetId"]).iterate_items()
)
print(f"Fetched {len(items)} stories")

Each item gives you the title, URL, score, author, comment count, and timestamp — everything you need for analysis.
Save each run to a local database so you can track changes over time:
import sqlite3

db = sqlite3.connect("hn_pipeline.db")
db.execute(
    "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS stories ("
    "id INTEGER, title TEXT, url TEXT, score INTEGER, "
    "comments INTEGER, author TEXT, fetched_at TEXT, "
    "PRIMARY KEY (id, fetched_at))"
)

fetched_at = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
for item in items:
    db.execute(
        "INSERT OR IGNORE INTO stories VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?)",
        (item["id"], item["title"], item.get("url", ""),
         item["score"], item.get("descendants", 0),
         item["by"], fetched_at)
    )
db.commit()

With a few days of data, you can spot rising topics:

title: Do commit ao deploy: CI/CD de uma API na AWS usando GitHub Actions, ECS e Terraform
summary: This comprehensive guide walks through building a complete CI/CD pipeline from code commit to production deployment on AWS using GitHub Actions, Terraform, and ECS Fargate. It demonstrates language-agnostic infrastructure-as-code patterns and secure OIDC authentication without static credentials. A merge to main automatically deploys containerized applications to production with full automation.

Colocar uma aplicação em produção vai muito além de escrever código. Envolve compilar, testar, empacotar e entregar de forma confiável e repetível. Neste artigo, vou mostrar como construir uma pipeline completa — do commit ao deploy — usando GitHub Actions para CI/CD, Terraform para infraestrutura como código e AWS (ECR, ECS Fargate) como plataforma de execução.
O conceito apresentado aqui é agnóstico de linguagem — funciona para qualquer stack que rode em um container Docker (Node.js, Go, Java, Python, etc.). O objetivo é demonstrar como essas ferramentas se conectam para formar um fluxo automatizado onde um simples merge na branch main resulta em uma nova versão rodando em produção, sem intervenção manual.

title: How to find missing i18n keys without losing your mind (or your job)
summary: The Localization Scanner VS Code extension helps developers detect missing i18n translation keys and hardcoded strings that should be localized. It scans code, identifies broken references, finds forgotten translation wrappers, and navigates directly to problem lines. The tool prevents the embarrassing production bug where users see raw translation key placeholders instead of actual UI text.
content:
Look, we’ve all been there. You just pushed a massive feature, you’re feeling like a genius, and then the "urgent" Slack message hits.
Your manager sends a screenshot from the production site, and instead of a nice "Submit" button, the users are seeing:
{{ settings.labels.confirm_action_final_v2_FIXED }}
It’s embarrassing. It looks like the code is leaking. And honestly, trying to keep your source code and your en.json file in sync manually is a one-way ticket to burnout. I got tired of the manual "Find in Files" dance, so I built a tool to handle it.
Introducing Localization Scanner
I created this VS Code extension because I wanted something that stayed out of the way until I actually needed it. It’s basically a safety net for your i18n workflow.
What it actually does for you:
Finds the "Ghosts": It scans your code and tells you exactly which keys you used that don't actually exist in your translation files.
The "Did I really type that?" Detector: It finds hardcoded strings that you forgot to wrap in a translation function. It’s smart enough to know that className="mb-4" isn't a translation, but "Are you sure you want to delete this?" definitely is.
Respects your RAM: I hate extensions that slow down my IDE. This has zero performance impact because it only runs when you hit the refresh button.
Click and Fix: You see a missing key in the sidebar, you click it, and it takes you right to the line where you missed it.
Check it out
Marketplace
GitHub
Please download it, use it, and leave a nice review. If I see a 5-star rating, I might actually finish my other 14 pending side projects. If you leave a bad one, I’m telling my mom. lol. But seriously, your feedback keeps me going! 
Let me know if it saves you from a "broken UI" bug report. I'd love to hear your feedback or see a PR if you want to add more features!


  
  
  vscodextensions #i18n #webdev #typescript #javascript #programming

zh.title: 为什么WebSocket中间件是可扩展实时应用的秘密武器
zh.summary: WebSocket中间件通过将原始套接字混乱转变为生产级系统，将消息路由到处理程序，跟踪连接健康状态，检测陈旧数据并强制执行内存限制。没有中间件保护措施（如有界数组），浏览器在数小时的实时数据流后会崩溃。适当的架构使系统能够处理数百万交易量。

fr.title: Pourquoi WebSocket Middleware Est l'Arme Secrète Derrière les Applications Temps Réel Évolutives
fr.summary: Le middleware WebSocket transforme le chaos des sockets bruts en un système de grade production en acheminant les messages vers les gestionnaires, en suivant la santé des connexions, en détectant les données obsolètes et en appliquant les limites de mémoire. Sans les protections du middleware comme les tableaux limités, les navigateurs s'arrêtent après des heures de streaming de données en temps réel. Une architecture appropriée permet aux systèmes de gérer des millions en volume de trading.

de.title: Warum WebSocket-Middleware die Geheimwaffe für skalierbare Echtzeit-Apps ist
de.summary: WebSocket-Middleware verwandelt rohes Socket-Chaos in ein produktionsreifes System, indem es Nachrichten an Handler weitergeleitet, die Verbindungsintegrität überwacht, veraltete Daten erkennt und Speichergrenzen durchsetzt. Ohne Middleware-Schutzmaßnahmen wie begrenzte Arrays stürzen Browser nach Stunden des Echtzeit-Datenstreamings ab. Angemessene Architektur ermöglicht Systemen, Millionen im Handelsvolumen zu bewältigen.

es.title: Por Qué WebSocket Middleware Es el Arma Secreta Detrás de Aplicaciones en Tiempo Real Escalables
es.summary: WebSocket middleware transforma el caos de socket bruto en un sistema de grado de producción enrutando mensajes a manejadores, rastreando la salud de la conexión, detectando datos obsoletos e imponiendo límites de memoria. Sin protecciones de middleware como matrices acotadas, los navegadores se cuelgan después de horas de transmisión de datos en tiempo real. La arquitectura adecuada permite que los sistemas manejen millones en volumen comercial.

title: How Do I Let My AI Agent Review My GitHub Repositories
summary: A Cloud Architect became a bottleneck reviewing infrastructure changes across multiple teams and automated the process using an AI assistant integrated with their codebase. The solution accelerates architectural reviews, infrastructure validation, and security approvals without manual delays. This pattern enables faster team velocity while maintaining quality oversight.
content:
👋 Hey there, tech enthusiasts! 
I'm Sarvar, a Cloud Architect with a passion for transforming complex technological challenges into elegant solutions. With extensive experience spanning Cloud Operations (AWS & Azure), Data Operations, Analytics, DevOps, and Generative AI, I've had the privilege of architecting solutions for global enterprises that drive real business impact. Through this article series, I'm excited to share practical insights, best practices, and hands-on experiences from my journey in the tech world. Whether you're a seasoned professional or just starting out, I aim to break down complex concepts into digestible pieces that you can apply in your projects.
Let's dive in and explore the fascinating world of cloud technology together! 🚀
When I became a Cloud Architect, I thought my bottleneck days were over. I assumed my role would shift from reviewing pull requests to designing scalable systems.
I was wrong.
Currently, I lead two different teams within the same project, each responsible for managing its own infrastructure requirements:
Team A: Migrating a legacy monolithic application to microservices on Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)
Team B: Building a secure and scalable CI/CD platform
Both teams required architectural guidance, infrastructure reviews, security approvals, and cost validation. And naturally, most of those decisions came to me. Within three months, I realized I had become a bottleneck again. But this time, it was worse.
Previously, my delays affected a single team. Now, they were affecting two different teams working in parallel. Every architectural review, infrastructure change, and security approval had to pass through me.
Something had to change. And this time, I had the authority to change it.
As a Senior DevOps Engineer, I could only optimize my own workflow. As a Cloud Architect, I could design a system for everyone.
I started asking questions:
What if I had an AI assistant that understood our entire codebase?
